title: Queen of puddings
subtitle: Breadcrumb custard baked with jam and crowned with meringue.
excerpt: A soft baked custard enriched with breadcrumbs and lemon, topped with a layer of jam and finished with a billowing soft meringue browned in the oven. An old-fashioned British pudding that is considerably better than it sounds. Serves four to six.
sourceNotes: Queen of puddings. The recipe is documented in British cookbooks from the mid-19th century under various names including 'Queen's pudding' and 'Bread pudding with meringue'. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) includes the recipe. The combination of baked custard, jam and soft meringue is a Victorian-era dessert that survived into 20th-century school and institutional cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the custard layer the day before; add jam and meringue just before baking.
recipe.temperatureNote: Meringue is browned at 200°C for the final 10 minutes.

content.text: Queen of puddings is three layers cooked in sequence: a breadcrumb custard set in the oven, a layer of warmed jam spread on top, and a soft meringue piled on and browned briefly at high heat. Each layer is simple; the combination is exceptional. The breadcrumbs absorb the custard and give the base a texture somewhere between a baked custard and a bread pudding.
Make and bake the custard base

Heat {{whole-milk}} of milk and {{unsalted-butter}} of butter together until the butter melts. Pour over {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs and leave to soak for 10 minutes. Stir in {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, the zest of {{lemon}} lemon, and {{eggs}} egg yolks (reserve the whites). Pour into a buttered baking dish and bake at 150°C for 25 to 30 minutes until just set and barely wobbly.
Add jam and meringue, then brown

Warm {{jam}} of jam gently until spreadable and spoon over the set custard. Whisk the reserved egg whites with a hand mixer until stiff peaks form. Gradually add {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and whisk to a glossy meringue. Pile on top of the jam layer. Bake at 200°C for 8 to 10 minutes until the meringue peaks are lightly browned. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Meringue added before custard was fully cooled.
items.fix: The custard base should be just set and cooling before the jam and meringue go on. A very hot base creates condensation under the meringue.

items.symptom: Custard base too liquid after baking
items.fix: Bake until the centre is just set with a very slight wobble. It firms further on resting.

items.symptom: Meringue egg whites will not stiffen
items.cause: A trace of yolk or grease in the whites or bowl.
items.fix: Separate the eggs carefully and use a scrupulously clean, dry bowl. Wipe with half a lemon and pat dry if in doubt.

content.text: Queen of puddings is a Victorian recipe that deserves a wider following than it has. The combination of soft custard, sharp jam and billowing meringue is well-balanced, and the whole pudding is made from things most people already have. It is British baked pudding at its most elegant: nothing exotic, nothing difficult, just good technique and a proper oven.
